explanations.1: This checks if the Apache server has the mod_deflate module installed, so we only run these compression rules if that module is available.
explanations.3: This is a nested check for mod_filter, which allows us to compress specific file types instead of everything.
explanations.4: This line tells Apache to compress the response for all these specific file types (like JavaScript, CSS, fonts, images) when they're sent to the browser.
explanations.10: This turns on the gzip compression module; 'Yes' is just how mod_gzip wants you to enable it.
explanations.11: This tells mod_gzip to break apart chunked responses so it can compress them properly.
explanations.12: This tells mod_gzip to compress files matching those extensions (html, txt, css, js, etc.) based on their filenames.
explanations.13: This tells mod_gzip to also compress responses that are generated by CGI scripts.
explanations.14: This is a pattern that says 'compress anything whose MIME type starts with text/' like text/html or text/plain.
explanations.20: This explicitly says 'don't compress image files' because images are usually already compressed and re-compressing wastes CPU.
explanations.21: This is an exception to line 20: SVG images should still be compressed because they're actually text-based, not binary like PNG or JPEG.
explanations.22: This prevents re-compressing content that's already been gzipped, which would waste server resources.
